full_response:
We take the instant at which the student discovers the problem to be  
t = 0 in the laboratory (MIT) frame.  
The microbes survive only 3 h of their own proper time τ ; we must therefore find the lab–frame
time t at which

  τ(t)=3 h.

Because the samples are whirling in a centrifuge, their instantaneous speed (measured in the
laboratory frame) is

  v(t)=v_0√(1−t/5)    ( t in hours, 0≤t≤5 )

Part (a) (not reproduced here) showed that v_0 must be 0.8 c so that, if the speed could be
held constant, 5 laboratory hours would correspond to 3 proper hours:

  constant–speed relation  τ = t√(1−β²) ⇒ (3 h)/(5 h)=√(1−β²) ⇒ β=0.8.

Hence

  β(t)=v(t)/c = 0.8 √(1−t/5).

1.  Proper–time element  
 dτ = dt √(1−β²) = dt √(1−0.8²(1−t/5))
  = dt √[1 − 0.64(1−t/5)]
  = dt √[1 − 0.64 + 0.64(t/5)]
  = dt √[0.36 + 0.128 t].

2.  Accumulated proper time  
 τ(t) = ∫₀ᵗ dt' √(0.36 + 0.128 t').

 Use ∫ √(a + bt) dt = (2/3b)(a + bt)^{3/2} :

 τ(t) = (2 / 3·0.128) [ (0.36 + 0.128 t)^{3/2} − (0.36)^{3/2} ]  
    = (2 / 0.384) [ (0.36 + 0.128 t)^{3/2} − 0.216 ]  
    = 5.208 [ (0.36 + 0.128 t)^{3/2} − 0.216 ]      (1)

3.  Set τ(t)=3 h and solve for t :

 3 = 5.208[ (0.36 + 0.128 t)^{3/2} − 0.216 ]  
 ⇒ (0.36 + 0.128 t)^{3/2} = 0.216 + 3 / 5.208  
            = 0.216 + 0.576 = 0.792

 Raise both sides to the 2/3 power:

 0.36 + 0.128 t = 0.792^{2/3} ≈ 0.856.

 Hence 0.128 t = 0.856 − 0.36 = 0.496  
     t = 0.496 / 0.128 ≈ 3.875 h.

Rounded to two decimal places,

\boxed{t_{\text{death}} \approx 3.88\ \text{hours (laboratory time)}}  

Therefore the microbes perish about 3.9 h after t = 0—well before the 5-hour delivery arrives.  

Confidence: 88%
